dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 75 for breathing problems at the dialysis center in Richmond today at 09:20.
Hi, I'm M75. What made you call 911 today?
The patient is having shortness of breath. It started halfway through her dialysis today.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name?
Sandra Smith.
Great, can you follow my finger with your eyes?
Yes, fine.
Can you squeeze my hand for me?
Yes, I can do that.
Are you alert and oriented to person, place, and time today?
Yes, I am Sandra, doing dialysis in Richmond today at about 09:15.
Excuse me, I need to check for any neck vein swelling or tracheal deviation. Can I inspect your neck now?
yes, go ahead.
I note no jugular venous distension or tracheal deviation observed at this time.
Ma'am, the bystander said you are having shortness of breath. Can you describe what is happening and how long it's been going on for you today?
Yes, I've been short of breath for about 15 minutes now while in the dialysis chair. I don't feel chest pain or any other symptoms.
Has your skin felt warm or looked pink, or is there a change in color with the shortness of breath?
My skin is still warm, pink, and dry-it hasn't changed from before this happened.
Pulse is 85 per minute, regular rhythm and normal quality.
Your respiratory rate is 23 breaths a minute, and you're breathing without accessory muscle use or signs of struggle.
Blood pressure is 172 over 84 millimeters of mercury.
I'm going to check blood glucose now. The result is 179 mg/dl.
SpO2 is 100% on room air.
Glasgow Coma Scale score is 15-showing that you're fully alert and responding appropriately at this point.
Your airway is clear. Can you speak clearly without difficulty or hoarseness now?
Yes, speech is clear.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how severe would you rate the discomfort you're feeling for the shortness of breath today?
I would rate that 0 today because it's not painful, just uncomfortable.
We noted shortness of breath as the only current symptom. Any fever, nausea, or dizziness at the moment?
Not that I could manage to think about. Just breath work and maybe a racing thought or two.
It's shortness of breath, right? So we're treating for that.
Let's check your head and neck. I'll start with the eyes and scan for any obvious issues.
Feel free to look. Just not sure if I can feel anything but my respiration.
I examined your head and neck again. I did not notice any injury or swelling, and I found no signs of JVD.
Can I place a stethoscope gently on your chest now to listen to your breath sounds better?
Please go ahead.
Your chest rise and fall is equal, breath sounds are clear in all lobes, no adventitious sounds or crackles present at this reading.
Moving to your legs, I'll check peripheral pulses now to evaluate arterial blood circulation. You okay with that?
Yep, go ahead.
Peripheral pulses are strong and symmetrical. I do not note any abnormalities in your extremities or back at this time.
I'm going to take a second blood glucose to recheck. Reading is now interfering with dialysis line residue.
Your breathing has calmed to 15 breaths a minute.
Your blood pressure is now 150 over 64 mmHg.
SpO2 is now 93%. I'll monitor that carefully.
Let's place a 4-lead monitor on you to get a reading of your EKG while I prepare a few materials.
Getting the leads placed now for some additional monitoring to be safe and precise.
4-lead monitoring is now fully on without issue.
Next, we'll place a 12-lead ECG on your chest to rule out cardiac involvement as a safety step.
Leads are in contact and stable, 12-lead tracing complete without complications occurring.
As per protocol, I will now place and administer a nasal cannula to give you 1 liter per minute of supplemental oxygen.
I'll keep that low flow to be gentle and safe for you as we do our transport.
Breathing has improved slightly with the oxygen support to 13 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is now 159 over 66-very similar to the last measured value, still stable for now.
SpO2 has improved to 98%-that's good. Your oxygen is matching well with the introduced nasal cannula.
Pulse is steady and currently at 80 beats per minute with no irregular patterns observed.
You previously mentioned some blood work at dialysis showed a low hemoglobin of 8.6. Correct?
Yes, the dialysis center ran a blood test and the result showed that my hemoglobin is 8.6.
Your record indicates allergies to morphine, naltrexone, and lisinopril, is that accurate for you now?
That's correct. I've had those confirmed in the past during dialysis pre-treatment checks.
You are currently taking clonidine. Correct?
Yes, that is accurate. I was confirmed for that during the routine check.
Sandra, let me confirm your past medical history-do you have a history of heart attacks, COPD, diabetes, asthma, high blood pressure, and heart failure?
Yes, exactly. I was told once I was having a heart attack in the past and about the COPD diagnosis, asthma is also valid, and yes, all of those I know about.
And do you have a history of primary hypertension or anemia?
Yes, I've had high blood pressure most of my adult life and low hemoglobin too since my dialysis.
Based on the respiratory distress, breathing difficulty, and low hemoglobin, you will be transported under the respiratory distress protocol as per protocol 3-11.
Would you be comfortable being transported to Chippenham Hospital, as it is the closest during your dialysis?
Yes, that is fine with me. That is the nearest and appropriate hospital.
Good. We will secure your dialysis chair in transfer to the stretcher while using a 5-point harness for your safety.
The transfer is complete and stable, and the harness is in place.
Sandra, moving forward, our transfer to the ambulance will be smooth and stable. Any specific issues with your neck or back today we should be careful about?
No, nothing in that area I notice or feel right now.
Great, I'll get you into the ambulance now so we can head to the hospital.
I'll get the wheel out to activate the stretcher transfer. All right, we're set. This will slide in.
I'll secure you with a 5-point harness and confirm seat belt placement before we head out.
Transfer was smooth and confirmed secured on the stretcher with the 5-point harness in place during the ambulance entry. All systems normal.
Patient is stable with no adverse events during transport.
